• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Profit Optimization

Ensemble Models and Bayesian Workflow

Andrej Arpáš

2026-07-17

Problem Setup

Imagine you’re optimizing (maximizing) for profit of some small business. The business has corporate clients (other corporate entities) and you have to decide which are to be targeted in a marketing campaign, the result of which should be a purchase decision on the part of the client. You target them by making a phone call but your call center can only handle calling 1/4 of the client base max. You have two datasets. One with the client base, and one called the pilot (which contains but a subset of the clients). Both contain 20 variables; the former requires making a recommendation (1 or 0) as to whom to target for the purchase, the latter also includes the purchase decisions and provides information about which clients, included in the pilot, were in the treatment group. Other than that, you know nothing about the variables.

Economically, contacting a client costs 5 and a purchase yields incremental discounted CLV of 100, so expected profit for contacting client \(i\) is approximately

\[ \mathbb{E}[\mathrm{profit}_i] = 100 \cdot P(\mathrm{purchase}_i \mid \mathrm{contact}) - 5. \]

Because untreated clients in the pilot still purchase at a non-trivial rate, ranking solely by unconditional \(P(\mathrm{purchase})\) can waste capacity on clients who would buy anyway. The cleaner targeting score is therefore either (i) \(P(\mathrm{purchase} \mid \mathrm{contact}=1)\), or (ii) an uplift score \(P(Y\mid T=1,X) - P(Y\mid T=0,X)\). We compare classical ML ensembles against a simple Bayesian logistic model, then allocate the call-center capacity (at most one quarter of the customer base) to the highest expected-profit scores.

One way to proceed is to use the pilot data as training and the client base data as the deployment set. I’ll run a number of machine learning models, which, considering we know nothing about the variables, I don’t expect to perform all that well, comparing that approach to a simple Bayesian model in terms of their predictive accuracy and efficacy of making recommendations about which clients to target. The Bayesian model will give us a posterior to work with to gauge the probabilities with which a client would make a purchase decision, which is what we will optimize for.

Okay, what we can do, to start off with, is to run some models to figure out what the variables (vars) were that turned out predictive of a purchase decision based on the pilot.

The idea being that optimizing/maximizing for those vars that perform best would yield us the best candidates for modelling on the remainder of the customer base. (Provided that the sample in pilot.csv is representative and statistically sound otherwise, which is one assumption here. If so, we can use it to make inferences about the whole lot.)

Data

A glance at what we’re dealing with.

pilot_path <- file.path(data_dir, "pilot.csv")
base_path <- file.path(data_dir, "CustomerBase.csv")

pilot <- readr::read_csv(pilot_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(V2, V19), as.factor),
    treatment = as.integer(treatment),
    purchase = as.integer(purchase)
  )

customer_base <- readr::read_csv(base_path, show_col_types = FALSE) |>
  mutate(across(c(V2, V19), as.factor))

# Align factor levels with the pilot (deployment set must share levels).
for (col in c("V2", "V19")) {
  levels(customer_base[[col]]) <- union(levels(pilot[[col]]), levels(customer_base[[col]]))
  customer_base[[col]] <- factor(customer_base[[col]], levels = levels(pilot[[col]]))
}

n_base <- nrow(customer_base)
call_capacity <- floor(capacity_frac * n_base)

pilot |>
  filter(treatment == 1) |>
  head() |>
  as_tibble()
# A tibble: 6 × 22
     V1 V2       V3    V4    V5    V6    V7    V8    V9   V10   V11    V12   V13
  <dbl> <fct> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>
1  0.76 A      0.11  0.44 36.7   0.28  47.0  0.38  1.67  5.5     18   27.8  0.68
2  1.09 A      0.42  0.16  2.04  0.37  36.7  0.49  0.7   9.27    10   31.4  0.51
3  0.69 A      0.24  0.87  6.02  0.3   44.0  0.56  2.39  9.47    19   48.2  0.68
4  0.98 A      0.62  0.93 31.2   1.83  44.8  0.81  4.51  8.1     19  704.   0.67
5  0.68 A      0.75  0.18 62.6   0.42  40.0  0.25  2.13  7.78    14   33.0  0.64
6  4.85 A      0.78  0.64 18.2   0.75  50.1  0.53  0.6  13.8     13 1730.   0.05
# ℹ 9 more variables: V14 <dbl>, V15 <dbl>, V16 <dbl>, V17 <dbl>, V18 <dbl>,
#   V19 <fct>, V20 <dbl>, treatment <int>, purchase <int>
pilot |>
  group_by(treatment) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    purchases = sum(purchase),
    purchase_rate = mean(purchase),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
# A tibble: 2 × 4
  treatment     n purchases purchase_rate
      <int> <int>     <int>         <dbl>
1         0  4003       315        0.0787
2         1   997       102        0.102

The control arm still purchases at roughly 7.9%, vs. about 10.2% when contacted. The treatment effect is therefore modest in the raw averages; any propensity model that ignores that fact is solving a slightly different problem than profit-maximizing targeting.

set.seed(42)

# Model the response among contacted clients for classical ML comparisons.
treated <- pilot |> filter(treatment == 1)

split_obj <- initial_split(treated, prop = 0.8, strata = purchase)
training <- training(split_obj)
testing <- testing(split_obj)

# Full pilot (with treatment) for uplift / Bayesian work.
pilot_model <- pilot |>
  mutate(purchase_f = factor(purchase, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes")))

EDA

We can attempt some visualizations but since we don’t know what the vars represent, there isn’t much to discern patterns-wise.

num_cols <- pilot |>
  select(where(is.numeric), -treatment) |>
  names()

corr <- cor(pilot[, num_cols], use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
corrplot(
  round(corr, 3),
  method = "square",
  type = "upper",
  tl.col = "black",
  order = "hclust",
  col = brewer.pal(n = 5, name = "RdBu")
)

chart1 <- ggplot(training, aes(x = V15, y = V7, color = factor(purchase))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(color = "purchase")

chart2 <- ggplot(training, aes(x = V15, y = V12, color = factor(purchase))) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(color = "purchase")

grid.arrange(chart1, chart2, ncol = 2)

We may do at least one ensemble model, so let’s do some prep for that.

ctrl <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 10)

# Drop near-constant / non-numeric helpers for the caret rpart path.
train_caret <- training |>
  select(-treatment) |>
  mutate(purchase = factor(purchase, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes")))

test_caret <- testing |>
  select(-treatment) |>
  mutate(purchase = factor(purchase, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes")))

out <- train(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = train_caret,
  method = "rpart2",
  tuneLength = 10,
  trControl = ctrl
)
note: only 3 possible values of the max tree depth from the initial fit.
 Truncating the grid to 3 .
plot(out)

So predictably, there are diminishing returns in using all vars. 15 max. should suffice but ideally way fewer.

plot(out$finalModel)
text(out$finalModel, pretty = 0)

y_hat <- predict(out, newdata = test_caret)
defaultSummary(data.frame(obs = test_caret$purchase, pred = y_hat))
   Accuracy       Kappa 
 0.84500000 -0.01706037

Virtually guaranteed to overfit in the training data, and underperform on the test data, by default; these results, however, seem pretty good on accuracy alone. That is mostly the base-rate effect: with sparse purchases, predicting “no” often looks strong. Confirming the hypothesis is the fact that performing tree pruning to hone in on “the best” parts of the original tree simply returns the root, so this seems already the most optimal tree under that criterion — which is a warning, not a victory.

Modelling

Let’s proceed to modelling and see if we can figure out what vars matter the most using some actual architectures. We can also do the modelling using tidymodels; it has a few extra wrinkles though, so let’s do the initial_split the tidymodels way to avoid clashes.

set.seed(42)

e <- treated |>
  select(-treatment) |>
  mutate(buybool = factor(purchase, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes"))) |>
  select(-purchase)

purchase_split <- initial_split(e, prop = 0.75, strata = buybool)
purchase_train <- training(purchase_split)
purchase_test <- testing(purchase_split)

base_recipe <- recipe(buybool ~ ., data = purchase_train) |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) |>
  step_zv(all_predictors()) |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

tree_model <- decision_tree(mode = "classification", cost_complexity = 0.006) |>
  set_engine("rpart")

tree_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(tree_model) |>
  add_recipe(base_recipe)
tree_fit <- fit(tree_wflow, data = purchase_train)

plot(tree_fit$fit$fit$fit)
text(tree_fit$fit$fit$fit, pretty = 0)

Get a confusion matrix implied by the model in the test set.

bind_cols(
  purchase = purchase_test$buybool,
  predict(tree_fit, new_data = purchase_test)
) |>
  conf_mat(truth = purchase, estimate = .pred_class)
          Truth
Prediction  no yes
       no  218  25
       yes   6   1

Nothing to write home about in terms of predictiveness so far but let’s proceed somewhat cautiously.

Random Forests (RF)

rf_model <- rand_forest(trees = 1000) |>
  set_engine("randomForest", importance = TRUE) |>
  set_mode("classification")

rf_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_model(rf_model) |>
  add_recipe(base_recipe)

cv <- vfold_cv(purchase_train, v = 5, strata = buybool)

fit_rf <- fit_resamples(
  rf_wflow,
  cv,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(fit_rf)
# A tibble: 4 × 6
  .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 accuracy binary     0.898     5 0.00106 pre0_mod0_post0
2 roc_auc  binary     0.715     5 0.0275  pre0_mod0_post0
3 sens     binary     1         5 0       pre0_mod0_post0
4 spec     binary     0         5 0       pre0_mod0_post0
rf_fit <- fit(rf_wflow, data = purchase_train)

bind_cols(
  purchase = purchase_test$buybool,
  predict(rf_fit, new_data = purchase_test)
) |>
  conf_mat(truth = purchase, estimate = .pred_class)
          Truth
Prediction  no yes
       no  224  26
       yes   0   0
vip(extract_fit_engine(rf_fit), aesthetics = list(fill = "#FF3366", alpha = 0.7)) +
  theme_minimal()

NULL

The predictiveness is weak and the results confirm what the visualizations intimated all along, which is that these vars do not seem terribly predictive of the purchase decision — conditional on the model and the data, that is. Since we want an ensemble model, we grudgingly oblige but I’m not happy about this. That said, let’s do Boosting, and see if we can do better.

Boosting

We still try to minimize a classification loss, but boosting builds an additive sequence of weak learners on reweighted residuals rather than bagging independent trees.

boost_model <- boost_tree(trees = 500, learn_rate = 0.05, tree_depth = 4) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("classification")

boost_wflow <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(base_recipe) |>
  add_model(boost_model)

fit_boost <- fit_resamples(
  boost_wflow,
  cv,
  metrics = metric_set(accuracy, roc_auc, sens, spec),
  control = control_resamples(save_pred = TRUE)
)

collect_metrics(fit_boost)
# A tibble: 4 × 6
  .metric  .estimator  mean     n std_err .config        
  <chr>    <chr>      <dbl> <int>   <dbl> <chr>          
1 accuracy binary     0.890     5 0.00743 pre0_mod0_post0
2 roc_auc  binary     0.761     5 0.0283  pre0_mod0_post0
3 sens     binary     0.976     5 0.00549 pre0_mod0_post0
4 spec     binary     0.133     5 0.0471  pre0_mod0_post0
boost_fit <- fit(boost_wflow, data = purchase_train)

bind_cols(
  purchase = purchase_test$buybool,
  predict(boost_fit, new_data = purchase_test)
) |>
  conf_mat(truth = purchase, estimate = .pred_class)
          Truth
Prediction  no yes
       no  220  25
       yes   4   1
vip(extract_fit_engine(boost_fit), aesthetics = list(fill = "#0099CC", alpha = 0.6)) +
  theme_minimal()

NULL

While this is technically an improvement on ROC-AUC in many runs, hard classification at 0.5 remains pretty abysmal because of class imbalance. That said, we can finally move on to more interesting models.

Discriminant Analysis (DA)

Nearest Shrunken Centroids and BART were explored in earlier drafts; they did not beat simpler discriminant analysis under the same treated-only split, so we keep the stronger classical baseline here.

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)

# LDA/QDA need stable within-class covariance; drop high-cardinality / near-constant
# categoricals (V2, V19) and keep numeric predictors only.
g <- treated |>
  select(-treatment, -V2, -V19) |>
  mutate(purchase = factor(purchase, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes")))

nzv <- nearZeroVar(g)
if (length(nzv) > 0) {
  g <- g[, -nzv]
}

set.seed(42)
g_split <- initial_split(g, prop = 0.75, strata = purchase)
g_train <- training(g_split)
g_test <- testing(g_split)
LDA <- train(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = g_train,
  method = "lda",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  trControl = trainControl(method = "none")
)
confusionMatrix(predict(LDA, newdata = g_test), reference = g_test$purchase)
Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  no yes
       no  223  26
       yes   1   0
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.892           
                 95% CI : (0.8468, 0.9276)
    No Information Rate : 0.896           
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.6313          
                                          
                  Kappa : -0.0078         
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 3.86e-06        
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.9955          
            Specificity : 0.0000          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.8956          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.0000          
             Prevalence : 0.8960          
         Detection Rate : 0.8920          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.9960          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.4978          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : no

Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)

QDA <- train(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = g_train,
  method = "qda",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  trControl = trainControl(method = "none")
)
confusionMatrix(predict(QDA, newdata = g_test), reference = g_test$purchase)
Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  no yes
       no  183  13
       yes  41  13
                                          
               Accuracy : 0.784           
                 95% CI : (0.7278, 0.8334)
    No Information Rate : 0.896           
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.9999999       
                                          
                  Kappa : 0.2148          
                                          
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.0002386       
                                          
            Sensitivity : 0.8170          
            Specificity : 0.5000          
         Pos Pred Value : 0.9337          
         Neg Pred Value : 0.2407          
             Prevalence : 0.8960          
         Detection Rate : 0.7320          
   Detection Prevalence : 0.7840          
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.6585          
                                          
       'Positive' Class : no

These are often among the best classical results so far. There is significant sparseness in the dataset on account of only about 10% purchase decisions among the treated in the pilot. That doesn’t bode particularly well for models to learn on. Adding bells and whistles to fine-tune the QDA rarely shaves off more false negatives at a fixed 0.5 threshold. So we have our best classical performer for hard labels — but for targeting we care about ranking and expected profit, not the 0.5 cut.

Back to Purchases

Let’s use the best classical model to score the customer base, then compare against a Bayesian ranking rule that respects capacity.

# Refit QDA on all treated pilot rows (same feature set as g).
qda_features <- setdiff(names(g), "purchase")
h <- customer_base |>
  select(all_of(intersect(qda_features, names(customer_base))))

# Ensure factor columns present in g exist in h with matching levels.
for (col in names(h)) {
  if (is.factor(g[[col]])) {
    h[[col]] <- factor(as.character(h[[col]]), levels = levels(g[[col]]))
  }
}

QDA_full <- train(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = g,
  method = "qda",
  preProcess = c("center", "scale"),
  trControl = trainControl(method = "none")
)

qda_prob <- predict(QDA_full, newdata = h, type = "prob")[["yes"]]
qda_pred <- as.integer(qda_prob >= 0.5)

tibble(
  source = c("treated_pilot", "customer_base_qda_0.5"),
  purchase_rate = c(mean(treated$purchase), mean(qda_pred))
)
# A tibble: 2 × 2
  source                purchase_rate
  <chr>                         <dbl>
1 treated_pilot                 0.102
2 customer_base_qda_0.5         0.140

Does this look reasonable? Matching the pilot purchase rate at a 0.5 threshold is not evidence that targeting is calibrated for profit — it mostly shows that the classifier’s positive rate echoes the training prevalence. We assume that the pilot was properly randomized and that the sampling was representative of the lot. We further condition on the model and the rest of the data.

Seeing the model expects only a few thousand hard positives at 0.5 while our call center capacity is 1/4 of the client base (23,750), we can call far more than the hard-positive set. Provided that we have already paid for the capacity/bandwidth, we might as well use it up to the point where expected incremental profit stays non-negative. If we have more candidates than spots, we rank by posterior (or model) probability and take the top 23,750. Honestly, our output suggests that machine learning (ML) doesn’t really promote thinking if one stops at accuracy. It’s useful, but what you really want is to understand your variables, do some proper modelling, ideally Bayesian, and perform statistical data analysis on your variables to prune them and in order to make hypotheses. You can in fact do everything purely Bayesian and have better decisions. Which is exactly what I’m going to show you next. We know nothing about our variables though, so it won’t be as effective as it should but my guess is that it will still decide more coherently than hard-threshold ML. Okay, so next up, Bayes.

stan_glm

One of the simpler models that is well suited to classification tasks.

set.seed(42)

# Two-model uplift on the full pilot: P(Y|T=1,X) and P(Y|T=0,X).
feature_cols <- c(
  "V1", "V3", "V4", "V5", "V6", "V7", "V8", "V9", "V10", "V11",
  "V12", "V13", "V14", "V15", "V16", "V17", "V18", "V20"
)

df_t1 <- pilot |>
  filter(treatment == 1) |>
  select(purchase, all_of(feature_cols))

df_t0 <- pilot |>
  filter(treatment == 0) |>
  select(purchase, all_of(feature_cols))

corrplot(cor(df_t1), tl.cex = 0.7)

t_prior <- student_t(df = 7, location = 0, scale = 2.5)

fit_t1 <- stan_glm(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = df_t1,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  prior = t_prior,
  prior_intercept = t_prior,
  QR = TRUE,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  refresh = 0,
  seed = 42
)

fit_t0 <- stan_glm(
  purchase ~ .,
  data = df_t0,
  family = binomial(link = "logit"),
  prior = t_prior,
  prior_intercept = t_prior,
  QR = TRUE,
  chains = 4,
  iter = 2000,
  refresh = 0,
  seed = 42
)
print(summary(fit_t1, probs = c(0.05, 0.95)), digits = 2)

Model Info:
 function:     stan_glm
 family:       binomial [logit]
 formula:      purchase ~ .
 algorithm:    sampling
 sample:       4000 (posterior sample size)
 priors:       see help('prior_summary')
 observations: 997
 predictors:   19

Estimates:
              mean   sd    5%    95%
(Intercept)  0.69   2.19 -2.92  4.35
V1           0.19   0.09  0.04  0.33
V3           1.00   0.55  0.09  1.88
V4           2.97   0.55  2.08  3.89

V6          -0.10   0.12 -0.30  0.09
V7          -0.06   0.04 -0.12  0.00

V9           0.17   0.07  0.06  0.28
V10         -0.06   0.07 -0.17  0.05

V15          0.05   0.07 -0.06  0.16

           mean   sd   5%   95%
mean_PPD 0.10   0.01 0.08 0.12 

The mean_ppd is the sample average posterior predictive distribution of the outcome variable (for details see help('summary.stanreg')).

MCMC diagnostics
              mcse Rhat n_eff
(Intercept)   0.03 1.00 6596 
V1            0.00 1.00 7684 
V3            0.01 1.00 6589 
V4            0.01 1.00 6837 
V5            0.00 1.00 4251 
V6            0.00 1.00 7261 
V7            0.00 1.00 7630 
V8            0.01 1.00 5628 
V9            0.00 1.00 9057 
V10           0.00 1.00 7175 
V11           0.00 1.00 8578 
V12           0.00 1.00 9120 
V13           0.01 1.00 8339 
V14           0.00 1.00 2035 
V15           0.00 1.00 6533 
V16           0.01 1.00 7281 
V17           0.00 1.00 6608 
V18           0.01 1.00 7642 
V20           0.00 1.00 5753 
mean_PPD      0.00 1.00 4543 
log-posterior 0.09 1.00 1389 

For each parameter, mcse is Monte Carlo standard error, n_eff is a crude measure of effective sample size, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence Rhat=1).

Diagnostics

plot(fit_t1, pars = feature_cols[1:8])

mcmc_rhat(rhat(fit_t1)) + yaxis_text(hjust = 1)

loo_t1 <- loo(fit_t1, save_psis = TRUE, k_threshold = 0.7)
print(loo_t1)

Computed from 4000 by 997 log-likelihood matrix.

         Estimate   SE
elpd_loo   -281.0 19.7
p_loo        22.9  3.5
looic       562.0 39.3
------
MCSE of elpd_loo is 0.2.
MCSE and ESS estimates assume independent draws (r_eff=1).

All Pareto k estimates are good (k < 0.7).
See help('pareto-k-diagnostic') for details.

We can note that \(\hat{R}\) is close to 1 (< 1.01), meaning the Markov chains are in close agreement with one another. So this looks good. The PSIS-LOO result is reliable enough when Pareto \(k\) estimates stay below 0.7.

pred_t1 <- posterior_epred(fit_t1)
p_hat <- colMeans(pred_t1)
y_hat_class <- as.integer(p_hat >= 0.5)
y_true <- df_t1$purchase

acc <- mean(y_hat_class == y_true)
sens <- mean(y_hat_class[y_true == 1] == 1)
spec <- mean(y_hat_class[y_true == 0] == 0)

tibble(
  accuracy = acc,
  sensitivity = sens,
  specificity = spec,
  n = length(y_true)
)
# A tibble: 1 × 4
  accuracy sensitivity specificity     n
     <dbl>       <dbl>       <dbl> <int>
1    0.896      0.0980       0.987   997
caret::confusionMatrix(
  factor(y_hat_class, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes")),
  factor(y_true, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("no", "yes"))
)
Confusion Matrix and Statistics

          Reference
Prediction  no yes
       no  883  92
       yes  12  10
                                        
               Accuracy : 0.8957        
                 95% CI : (0.875, 0.914)
    No Information Rate : 0.8977        
    P-Value [Acc > NIR] : 0.608         
                                        
                  Kappa : 0.1297        
                                        
 Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 9.44e-15      
                                        
            Sensitivity : 0.98659       
            Specificity : 0.09804       
         Pos Pred Value : 0.90564       
         Neg Pred Value : 0.45455       
             Prevalence : 0.89769       
         Detection Rate : 0.88566       
   Detection Prevalence : 0.97793       
      Balanced Accuracy : 0.54232       
                                        
       'Positive' Class : no

Do note that (i), off the bat, the simplest Bayesian model is at least competitive with the ML stack on the treated sample, and (ii) hard 0.5 classification is still the wrong operating point for a capacity-constrained campaign. Therefore, let’s use posterior predictive means to rank the overall customer base instead.

# Score the deployment set with both arms; uplift = p1 - p0.
newdata_base <- customer_base |>
  select(all_of(feature_cols))

# Use posterior expectation of probability (faster and stabler than Bernoulli draws).
p1 <- colMeans(posterior_epred(fit_t1, newdata = newdata_base, draws = 1000))
p0 <- colMeans(posterior_epred(fit_t0, newdata = newdata_base, draws = 1000))

uplift <- p1 - p0
expected_profit <- clv_gain * uplift - contact_cost

score_tbl <- tibble(
  company_id = seq_len(n_base),
  p_contact = p1,
  p_control = p0,
  uplift = uplift,
  expected_profit = expected_profit
)

# Rank by expected incremental profit; fill capacity only while EV stays positive when possible.
ranked <- score_tbl |>
  arrange(desc(expected_profit), desc(p_contact)) |>
  mutate(rank = row_number())

n_positive_ev <- sum(ranked$expected_profit > 0)
n_target <- min(call_capacity, max(n_positive_ev, 0L))
if (n_target == 0L) {
  # Fallback: if no positive-EV rows under the uplift model, use top capacity by p_contact.
  n_target <- call_capacity
  ranked <- score_tbl |>
    arrange(desc(p_contact)) |>
    mutate(rank = row_number())
}

dec_final <- ranked |>
  mutate(decision = as.integer(rank <= n_target)) |>
  arrange(company_id) |>
  transmute(
    decision,
    company_name = company_id,
    prob = p_contact,
    uplift,
    expected_profit
  )

dec_final |>
  head(15) |>
  as_tibble()
# A tibble: 15 × 5
   decision company_name    prob   uplift expected_profit
      <int>        <int>   <dbl>    <dbl>           <dbl>
 1        0            1 0.0145  -0.00579          -5.58 
 2        0            2 0.00610 -0.00582          -5.58 
 3        0            3 0.00721  0.00558          -4.44 
 4        0            4 0.269   -0.0301           -8.01 
 5        0            5 0.0256   0.00204          -4.80 
 6        0            6 0.140    0.00399          -4.60 
 7        0            7 0.0976   0.00363          -4.64 
 8        1            8 0.188    0.0699            1.99 
 9        0            9 0.00961  0.00550          -4.45 
10        0           10 0.579   -0.0718          -12.2  
11        1           11 0.0995   0.0760            2.60 
12        1           12 0.153    0.0569            0.686
13        0           13 0.0242   0.0204           -2.96 
14        0           14 0.301    0.0305           -1.95 
15        0           15 0.0112   0.00836          -4.16 
c(
  n_base = n_base,
  call_capacity = call_capacity,
  n_positive_ev = n_positive_ev,
  n_targeted = sum(dec_final$decision),
  mean_p_contact_targeted = mean(dec_final$prob[dec_final$decision == 1]),
  mean_uplift_targeted = mean(dec_final$uplift[dec_final$decision == 1])
)
                 n_base           call_capacity           n_positive_ev 
           9.500000e+04            2.375000e+04            2.239600e+04 
             n_targeted mean_p_contact_targeted    mean_uplift_targeted 
           2.239600e+04            2.167172e-01            1.164384e-01 
out_path <- file.path(out_dir, format(Sys.Date(), "%Y%m%d_stan_uplift_profit.csv"))
readr::write_csv(dec_final |> select(decision), out_path)
readr::write_csv(dec_final, file.path(out_dir, "decisions_arpas.csv"))
out_path
[1] "outputs/20260717_stan_uplift_profit.csv"

So, if we want to do this “old school”, and specify a 0.5 probability threshold, we generate a binary outcome, purchase / no purchase, but that doesn’t necessarily make a whole lot of sense for a capacity-constrained call center. Furthermore, doing so gives us far fewer “buys” than the 23,750 slots we can fill. Indeed, we do Bayes because we want to avail ourselves of the power of posterior distributions and the probabilistic nature of the models. Therefore, what we do is look at posterior mean probabilities (and the implied uplift / expected profit), order them from highest to smallest, and identify up to 23,750 of the highest ones in descending order — stopping earlier if expected incremental profit turns negative.

Conclusion and Final Output

What we have is a data frame with the clients in the original customer-base order, with the recommended decision, the contact-response probability, the estimated uplift, and expected incremental profit adjacent, conditional on the data and the two-arm Bayesian model. Compared with hard-threshold ML, this is a much better approach for the actual profit objective: it uses the randomized pilot as an A/B design, targets on incremental lift rather than raw propensity alone, and respects the call-center capacity constraint.

If more time were available, natural extensions would include interaction-aware uplift forests, explicit cost-sensitive thresholds tuned on a held-out pilot fold, and hierarchical shrinkage across sparse categorical levels (e.g. V19).
